1: The apostolic co-working ('working together with him') is grounded in the ministry of reconciliation just defined (2 Corinthians 5:18-21). Paul and his team are co-laborers in Christ's ongoing reconciling work — the work of the one who 'reconciled us to himself through Christ' continues in apostolic proclamation. The appeal not to receive grace 'in vain' echoes the language of the Servant in Isaiah 49:4 ('I have labored in vain') — Christ as the Servant whose work could not fail means that the grace he accomplished cannot be emptied by the community's failure to respond. The appeal is urgent precisely because the reconciliation Christ achieved is real and available.
2: The 'favorable time' and 'day of salvation' from Isaiah 49:8 are now: Christ's death and resurrection have inaugurated the Servant's promised acceptable year. The eschatological 'now' (nyn) of the new covenant announces that the time for which Israel's prophets hoped has arrived in Christ. Every apostolic proclamation is a direct extension of the Servant's mission — Christ is both the fulfillment of the Isaianic acceptable-time and the one in whose name the apostle continues to declare it. The double 'now' (now is the acceptable time; now is the day of salvation) grounds the community's urgency in the Christological fulfillment of prophecy.
3: The concern to put 'no obstacle in the way of the gospel' identifies Paul's ministry conduct as subordinated to the message about Christ. The gospel's content (reconciliation through the crucified and risen Christ) must not be contradicted by the messenger's behavior. The Christological logic: a discredited apostle would suggest that the Christ he proclaims is likewise discredited. Authentic representation of the one who 'knew no sin' (2 Corinthians 5:21) requires a transparent ministry that does not invite accusations of corruption, exploitation, or deception.
4: The self-commendation 'as servants of God' through the hardship catalog places apostolic identity within the Christological pattern: the Christ who humbled himself and took the form of a servant (Philippians 2:7) defines what genuine divine service looks like. Paul's hardship list is not masochism but Christological: the servant-pattern is the form authentic ministry takes in the world that crucified its Lord. The Corinthians who prize Sophistic display are being shown what Christ-shaped ministry actually looks like — not rhetorical triumph but endured suffering for the sake of others.
5: The catalog of beatings, imprisonments, tumults, labors, sleepless nights, and hunger reads against the backdrop of Christ's own passion: beaten, imprisoned, subjected to a tumultuous trial, laboring under the cross, forsaken in darkness. Paul's apostolic life is a participation in the sufferings of Christ (2 Corinthians 1:5: 'as we share abundantly in Christ's sufferings'). The hardships are not incidental to the apostolic mission but are the form in which Christ's suffering continues to be embodied in the world through those who belong to him and carry his message.
6: The virtues list — purity, knowledge, patience, kindness, Holy Spirit, unhypocritical love, word of truth, power of God — describes the character of Christ-shaped ministry. Each virtue reflects something of Christ's own character: the purity of the sinless one, the knowledge that comes through Christ (2 Corinthians 3:14; 2 Corinthians 4:6), the patience (makrothymia) of the one who endured the cross's shame (Hebrews 12:2), the kindness of the Lord whose yoke is easy (Matthew 11:30). The Holy Spirit, through whom Christ distributes these virtues, is the link between Christ's character and the apostle's formed life.
7: The 'weapons of righteousness for the right and left hands' are the weapons of Christ's righteousness administered through apostolic ministry. In the Servant tradition (Isaiah 11:5: 'righteousness shall be the belt of his waist'), the Messiah is equipped with divine righteousness as his weapon. Paul wields not his own righteousness but Christ's — the same righteousness that the Corinthians have been made to be (2 Corinthians 5:21: 'the righteousness of God'). The apostolic defensive and offensive arming is a participation in the Messiah's own righteous warfare against sin, death, and the powers.
8: The antithetical pairs — honor/dishonor, slander/praise, deceivers/truthful — describe the mixed social reception of Christ himself projected onto his apostle. Christ was honored at his triumphal entry and dishonored at his crucifixion; praised as Messiah and slandered as a blasphemer; called a deceiver (Matthew 27:63) and recognized as the Truth (John 14:6). The apostle's paradoxical social position is the continuation of the Christ-pattern: rejected by some, received by others, consistently misunderstood by those who evaluate by worldly standards.
9: The dying-yet-alive paradox of 2 Corinthians 6:9 is the resurrection pattern projected into the apostolic present. Christ was 'known' (acknowledged) and 'unknown' (rejected and misunderstood); he truly died and truly lives; he was disciplined (in the sense of suffering as the innocent one) yet not killed in any ultimate sense. Paul's apostolic experience is a participation in the dying-and-rising pattern of Christ: not mere imitation but genuine incorporation into the pattern of the crucified and risen one, whose death Paul carries in the body (2 Corinthians 4:10) and whose life is manifested through that death.
10: The 'having nothing yet possessing everything' is the eschatological reversal that Christ's resurrection inaugurates. The one who had 'nowhere to lay his head' (Matthew 8:20) and was crucified in naked poverty possesses 'all things' (1 Corinthians 3:22-23; Colossians 1:15-20) as the risen and exalted Lord. Paul participates in both the poverty (literal economic vulnerability) and the possessing-all (the eschatological riches of the new creation that belong to those who are 'in Christ'). Christian poverty is not ultimate loss but participatory sharing in Christ's own redemptive movement through poverty to resurrection-wealth.
11: Paul's wide-open heart toward the Corinthians reflects the character of Christ's own open-hearted mission: the one who welcomed all who came to him, who 'opened wide his mouth' in proclamation (cf. Psalms 81:10 LXX, cited with 'open your mouth and I will fill it'), whose heart was not restricted even toward those who rejected him. The apostle's emotional transparency (speaking 'plainly' — Corinthians, our mouth is open to you) is modeled on the directness of the Shepherd who calls his sheep by name and whose voice they recognize.
12: The Corinthians' emotional narrowness toward Paul is a form of their narrowness toward Christ whom Paul represents. To restrict one's heart toward the apostle of reconciliation is to restrict it toward the reconciliation Christ accomplished. The relational repair Paul is urging has Christological stakes: the opened heart toward the apostle corresponds to the opened heart toward the one who 'stands at the door and knocks' (Revelation 3:20 — to the Laodicean church, but applicable as a pattern). The Corinthians' return to Paul is the measure of their return to the Christ Paul embodies.
13: The father-children metaphor for apostolic relationship is rooted in Christ's own teaching about the heavenly Father and his children (Matthew 6:9; John 20:17: 'my Father and your Father'). The apostle's fatherly appeal for widened hearts participates in the divine Father's own appeal to his people throughout Israel's history — an appeal the Son embodied in the incarnation. The request for reciprocal heart-widening is ultimately a request to receive Christ's own reconciling work fully: to let the love that was poured out through the cross do its complete work in the community's emotional life.
14: The prohibition against being unequally yoked with unbelievers is a Christological boundary: the community belongs to Christ, not to idols. The series of rhetorical contrasts (Christ/Beliar; believer/unbeliever; temple of God/idols) maps onto the cosmic conflict between Christ and the powers that Paul has described throughout. Christ's victory over the principalities and powers (Colossians 2:15) creates a community that belongs exclusively to him — participation in idol-cult worship would be a denial of that belonging, a practical assertion that Christ's lordship does not extend to every area of life.
15: The Christ/Beliar contrast is the eschatological conflict made concrete for the Corinthian community. Christ's exorcism ministry (Mark 1:21-28; 2 Corinthians 3:11-12) demonstrated his authority over all demonic powers; his death and resurrection completed the decisive defeat (John 12:31: 'now the ruler of this world will be cast out'). The community that belongs to the victorious Christ cannot share allegiance with the defeated powers. The absolute incompatibility Paul asserts is not cultural scrupulosity but Christological: Christ's lordship and Beliar's lordship are mutually exclusive, and the community must live in the reality of Christ's victory.
16: The 'temple of God' which the community is (cf. 1 Corinthians 3:16; 2 Corinthians 6:19) is the Christological transformation of the Jerusalem temple. Christ's body is the true temple (John 2:21: 'he was speaking about the temple of his body'); through union with him, the community becomes the dwelling-place of God in the Spirit. The covenant-formula citations that follow ('I will dwell in them... I will be their God') are the promises that Jesus claimed to fulfill when he cleansed the temple and declared it his Father's house. In Christ, the community is the new temple where God dwells among his people.
17: The call to 'come out and be separate' cites Isaiah 52:11 — the oracle that precedes the Fourth Servant Song (Isaiah 52:13-53:12). The Servant whose suffering is about to be described in Isa 53 is preceded by a call to the exilic community to depart from Babylon in purity. Christ is both the Servant whose suffering Isa 53 describes and the one whose departure from sin's domain (his incarnation as the sinless one who 'came out' from the Father, John 8:42) creates the community that is called out with him. Separation from idolatry is not legalism but participation in Christ's own holiness.
18: The expanded covenant-formula — 'I will be a father to you and you will be sons and daughters' — is fulfilled in Christ, who is the Son of God through whom believers become sons and daughters (Galatians 4:4-7: God sent his Son... so that we might receive adoption as sons). The universal sonship-and-daughterhood Paul envisions (adding daughters to the original David-oracle) is grounded in the one Son who shares his Sonship with all who are united to him. The family of God is constituted by the Son's mediating work: because Christ is the Son, all who are in him share his filial relationship with the Father.
